revision.comment: Created page with "'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to expl..."
revision.text:
'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: * {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} * {{ill|Five Elements theory|ja|五行思想}} * Practical Applications: * {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} * {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} * {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: * {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}
[[ja:陰陽五行思想]] [[Category:Daoism]] [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Chinese cosmology]] [[Category:Five Elements]] [[Category:Japanese cultural history]]

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: * {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} * {{ill|Five Elements theory|ja|五行思想}} * Practical Applications: * {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} * {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} * {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: * {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}
[[ja:陰陽五行思想]] [[Category:Daoism]] [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Chinese cosmology]] [[Category:Five Elements]] [[Category:Japanese cultural history]] [[Category:new pages]][[category:original pages]]

revision.comment: capitalizing category because it messes with capitalization otherwise
revision.text:
'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: * {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} * {{ill|Five Elements theory|ja|五行思想}} * Practical Applications: * {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} * {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} * {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: * {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Five Elements theory|ja|五行思想}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."

The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5

Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.

== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory]] to [[Yin-Yang and Five Elements]]
revision.text:
'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Five Elements theory|ja|五行思想}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

[[ja:陰陽五行思想]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Daoism]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Chinese cosmology]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Five Elements]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese cultural history]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:last refresh attempt]] [[Category:Images]]

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|24 solar terms|ja|二十四節気}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

revision.text:
'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
