'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.

Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.

'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Yin-Yang Five Elements Theory" to "Yin-Yang and the Five Phases"
revision.text:
'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Five Elements Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)}}

<!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases-->

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).

Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichitsu|qq=}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daoist magic|qq=}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Pillars of Destiny|qq=}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mao (Manga)|qq=}}
