revision.comment: Text replacement - "陰陽五行思想|" to "陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|qq=}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar term|qq=}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichitsu|qq=}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daoist magic|qq=}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Pillars of Destiny|qq=}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mao (Manga)|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|version=101512574|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar term|qq=}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichitsu|qq=}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行思想|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|2=ja|3=五行思想|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行思想|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|2=ja|3=五行思想|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daoist magic|qq=}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行思想|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|2=ja|3=五行思想|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Pillars of Destiny|qq=}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mao (Manga)|qq=}}

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|qq=}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar term|qq=}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichitsu|qq=}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daoist magic|qq=}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Pillars of Destiny|qq=}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mao (Manga)|qq=}}

When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar term|qq=}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichitsu|qq=}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daoist magic|qq=}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Pillars of Destiny|qq=}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mao (Manga)|qq=}}

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|qq=}} and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}.
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|{{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|ja|干支|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sexagenary cycle|qq=}}.
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}}, around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the {{ill|solar term|ja|二十四節気|lt=24 solar terms|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar term|qq=}}, marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * {{ill|Lichun|ja|立春|1=Lichun|lt=Risshun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lichun|qq=}} (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * {{ill|Keichitsu|ja|啓蟄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichitsu|qq=}} (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * {{ill|Shōkan|ja|小寒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkan|qq=}} (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''{{ill|Guanzi|ja|管子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guanzi|qq=}}'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|qq=}} governance and gave rise to {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}}, incorporating {{ill|Daoist magic|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daoist magic|qq=}} and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** {{ill|Four Pillars of Destiny|ja|四柱推命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Pillars of Destiny|qq=}} ** {{ill|Feng Shui|ja|風水|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Feng Shui|qq=}} ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** {{ill|Mao (Manga)|ja|MAO (漫画)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mao (Manga)|qq=}}

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|[[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]]}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** [[:en:Four Pillars of Destiny|Four Pillars of Destiny]] ** [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** [[:en:Mao (manga)|Mao (Manga)]]

<!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--> [[Category:中国の名数5]] [[Category:Concepts in Chinese philosophy]]

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|[[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]]}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** [[:en:Four Pillars of Destiny|Four Pillars of Destiny]] ** [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** [[:en:Mao (manga)|Mao (Manga)]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|[[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]]}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=}} * Practical Applications: ** [[:en:Four Pillars of Destiny|Four Pillars of Destiny]] ** [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** [[:en:Mao (manga)|Mao (Manga)]]

{{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|[[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]]}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 灯籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}} * Practical Applications: ** [[:en:Four Pillars of Destiny|Four Pillars of Destiny]] ** [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** [[:en:Mao (manga)|Mao (Manga)]]
{{translated page|ja|陰陽五行思想|qq=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|version=101512574|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].

Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.

== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.

Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 灯籠流し}}.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11265234]] Wikidata name: yin-yang wu xing theory [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|[[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]]}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 灯籠流し}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=Yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想}} * Practical Applications: ** [[:en:Four Pillars of Destiny|Four Pillars of Destiny]] ** [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=}} * Cultural Depictions: ** [[:en:Mao (manga)|Mao (Manga)]]

'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.

=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想|1=Wu Xing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218652}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=|1=rekihou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514955}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 灯籠流し|1=water lantern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q391339}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.

== Related Articles == * Foundational Theories: ** {{ill|Yin-Yang theory|ja|陰陽思想|1=yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang theory|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q62744}} ** {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements theory|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想|1=Wu Xing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218652}} * Practical Applications: ** [[:en:Four Pillars of Destiny|Four Pillars of Destiny]] ** [[:en:Feng shui|Feng Shui]] ** {{ill|Acupuncture|ja|鍼灸|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acupuncture|qq=|1=acupuncture and moxibustion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11650808}} * Cultural Depictions: ** [[:en:Mao (manga)|Mao (Manga)]]

<!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--><!--Yin-Yang and the Five Phases--> [[Category:Chinese Enumerations of 5]] [[Category:Concepts in Chinese philosophy]] [[Category:Religious cosmologies]] [[Category:I Ching]] [[Category:Taoism]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:음양오행설]] {{wikidata link|Q11265234}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11265234]] Wikidata name: yin-yang wu xing theory [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I question the notability of this as its own concept, as opposed to just Wuxing and Ying Yang or something like that[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:24, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory''' (陰陽五行思想, ''In'yō Gogyo Shisō'') is a philosophy that combines the concepts of [[:en:Yin and yang|Yin and Yang]] and the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想|1=Wu Xing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218652}}, which originally developed independently in {{ill|China|ja|中国|1=China|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29520}} during the {{ill|Spring and Autumn period|ja|春秋戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring and Autumn period|qq=|1=Spring and Autumn Warring States Period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113995895}}. This theory, also known as ''In'yō Gogyo Setsu'' (陰陽五行説) or ''In'yō Gogyo Ron'' (陰陽五行論), was later unified to explain complex phenomena. It has also been employed in divination and other practices, such as in [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]].
== The Ten Heavenly Stems and Twelve Earthly Branches == {{Main|[[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]]}} The foundation of Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory lies in the five elements: wood (木), fire (火), earth (土), metal (金), and water (水), each paired with yin (陰) and yang (陽). These elements correspond to the ten heavenly stems: 甲, 乙, 丙, 丁, 戊, 己, 庚, 辛, 壬, and 癸, read in ''on'yomi'' as kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki.
When read in ''kun'yomi'', the pairs become ''kinoe'' (wood-yang), ''kinoto'' (wood-yin), ''hinoe'' (fire-yang), ''hinoto'' (fire-yin), and so forth, clearly reflecting their elemental associations. For instance, ''kinoe'' represents "wood's yang."
The twelve Earthly Branches also align with the elements based on seasonal cycles: * Spring: January (寅), February (卯), March (辰) – wood, wood, earth * Summer: April (巳), May (午), June (未) – fire, fire, earth * Autumn: July (申), August (酉), September (戌) – metal, metal, earth * Winter: October (亥), November (子), December (丑) – water, water, earth
The cyclic pairing of the heavenly stems and earthly branches produces 60 unique combinations (e.g., 甲寅 or 乙卯), forming the [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Sexagenary cycle]].
== Calendar == The Yin-Yang Five Elements calendar and its associated divination system consider the year beginning with [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]], around February 4 in the Gregorian calendar. Months start not on the first day but on one of the [[:en:Solar term|24 solar terms]], marking seasonal transitions.
Examples of monthly beginnings: * [[:en:Lichun|Risshun]] (Beginning of Spring): around February 4 * [[:en:Jingzhe|Keichitsu]] (Awakening of Insects): around March 6 * [[:en:Xiaohan|Shōkan]] (Lesser Cold): around January 5
During this calendar system, the year's zodiac changes at ''shōkan,'' so the period from January 5 to February 3 still belongs to the preceding year’s zodiac.
== Logic of Yin-Yang and Five Elements == The theory combines {{ill|Yin and Yang|ja|陰陽思想|1=yin and yang|lt=Yin-Yang Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and Yang|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin and yang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 陰陽|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q62744}} and {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想|1=Wu Xing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218652}} to explain the natural world. Its origins trace to ''The Four Seasons'' chapter in ''[[:en:Guanzi (text)|Guanzi]]'' and its framework relies on combinations derived from the ''I Ching''.
'''Yin and Yang''': These represent dual forces like positive and negative, light and dark, or male and female. Their interaction governs balance and cyclic transformation.
'''Five Elements''': Originating from ancient governance principles, the elements correspond to five planets: Mercury (water), Venus (metal), Mars (fire), Jupiter (wood), and Saturn (earth).
=== Five Elements Interactions === {{Main|{{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|lt=Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五行思想|1=Wu Xing|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218652}}}} Interactions are categorized as: * '''Mutual Generation (相生)''': Enhancing relationships, such as wood generating fire, which produces ash (earth), from which metal is extracted. * '''Mutual Restraint (相剋)''': Regulating relationships, such as water extinguishing fire, which melts metal.
== Influence in Japan == Introduced alongside {{ill|Calendrical systems|ja|暦法|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical systems|qq=|1=rekihou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514955}} in the 5th and 6th centuries, the theory influenced [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] governance and gave rise to [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]], incorporating [[:en:Taoism|Daoist magic]] and evolving into uniquely Japanese practices.
Seasonal customs like the New Year (春, associated with wood) and Bon Festival (秋, associated with water) reflect its cultural integration, influencing rituals like {{ill|Tōrō nagashi|ja|燈籠流し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōrō nagashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 灯籠流し|1=water lantern|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q391339}}.
== References == * Kobayashi Nobuaki, ''Research on Yin-Yang and Five Elements in Early China,'' Kodansha, 1951.
